hook: Sea cave whose mouth, seen from inside, frames the silhouette of Africa
facts: Sea opening is shaped uncannily like the African continent
Berbers quarried the walls for millstones, enlarging the natural cave
Stone-cutting circles and eye-shaped Phoenician marks survive on the rock
Sits 14 km west of Tangier below Cap Spartel
legend: Legend says Hercules slept here during his eleventh labour, fetching the golden apples of the Hesperides
They say he split the mountain to open the Strait of Gibraltar
w: Step inside and the sea-mouth resolves into the exact silhouette of Africa, turquoise water where the continent should float. Part natural, part hacked out by Berbers cutting millstones, the floor still scarred with their grinding circles. Legend says Hercules slept here. The map of a continent, carved by tides.

hook: Sultan's 1864 lighthouse marking the official western edge of the Mediterranean
facts: Built by Sultan Muhammad IV in 1864, Morocco's oldest lighthouse
Its longitude is the legally recognised western limit of the Mediterranean
Eleven nations once jointly funded its upkeep under treaty
Stands on a cliff at the Atlantic mouth of the Strait of Gibraltar
w: Where the Atlantic shoulders into the Mediterranean, a sultan raised this tower in 1864 and a year later handed its keys to eleven foreign powers who paid the bills. International law still draws the sea's western edge down this exact meridian. Morocco's oldest light, guarding two oceans at once.

hook: Terraced clifftop cafe over the strait, open since 1921
facts: Opened 1921, terraces stepping down the Marshan cliff toward Spain
Paul Bowles brought Burroughs, Kerouac and the Beats to its tables
The Rolling Stones and Brian Jones drank mint tea on these ledges
Sits beside the Phoenician necropolis above the Bay of Tangier
w: Stepped down a Marshan cliff since 1921, blue benches facing Spain across the strait. Paul Bowles held court here and dragged Burroughs and Kerouac up; later the Rolling Stones turned up for the mint tea and the view. Same glasses, same gnats, same hypnotic horizon. The whole Interzone's living room.

hook: America's first foreign property, gifted by a sultan, deep in the medina
facts: Morocco recognised US independence in 1777, first nation to do so
Sultan gave the building to America in 1821 as its first overseas property
Only US National Historic Landmark on foreign soil
A rambling mansion straddling both sides of Rue d'Amerique
w: Morocco recognised the young United States in 1777 before anyone else, and in 1821 a sultan handed Washington this rambling house off a medina alley. America's oldest diplomatic property anywhere, and its only landmark on foreign soil. Now a museum bridging an arch over Rue d'Amerique. Empire began in a souk.

hook: Spy-era cafe on Place de France, frozen since 1927
facts: Opened 1927 across from the French Consulate on Boulevard Pasteur
Eavesdropping agents worked the room during the International Zone years
Filmed in The Bourne Ultimatum and Bond's The Living Daylights
Bowles and Burroughs were regulars at its marble tables
w: Marble, mirrors and retired men nursing one coffee for three hours, unchanged since 1927. In the International Zone era spies sat reading newspapers they never turned, listening. Matt Damon sprinted past it in Bourne; Bond passed through too. Frozen mid-intrigue on Place de France. Order tea, watch the door.

hook: 1949 bookshop that set the tone for literary Tangier
facts: Founded 1949 by the Gerofi family as a Gallimard outpost
Bowles, Genet, Beckett, Yourcenar and Goytisolo all passed through
Yves Saint Laurent's Pierre Berge later bought and revived it
Still trades in four languages and runs its own imprint
w: Since 1949 the engine room of literary Tangier, where Bowles, Genet, Beckett and Goytisolo browsed the same shelves. The Gerofi sisters ran it as salon and switchboard for the whole expat set; Pierre Berge rescued it decades on. Four languages, one imprint, a city's memory in stock. Books as the local religion.

hook: Anglican church with a Moorish interior and Arabic scripture carved in stucco
facts: Built on land Sultan Hassan I gave the British, consecrated 1905
Interior carved with zellij, horseshoe arches and Arabic calligraphy
Belltower shaped like a minaret over the graveyard
Caid Sir Harry Maclean, who commanded the Moroccan army, lies buried here
w: A sultan gave the British the land, so they built an Anglican church and then carved the Lord's Prayer in Arabic above the altar, zellij and horseshoe arches throughout, a minaret for a belltower. In the yard sleeps Caid Maclean, the Scot who ran Morocco's army. Christendom in Moorish dress, eccentric and lovely.

hook: Governor's palace on the citadel's highest point, built over an English fort
facts: Raised in the early 1700s under Sultan Moulay Ismail
Built atop the ruins of the English garrison's Upper Castle
Courtyards of marble fountains, carved cedar ceilings and arabesque
A museum since 1922, overlooking the strait
w: On the citadel's crown, sultans raised this palace in the early 1700s squarely on the rubble of the English garrison they had just driven out. Marble courtyards, cedar ceilings, a Pasha's swagger above the strait. A museum since 1922, now full of the city's older selves. Power built on the wreck of power.

hook: 1870 harbour-side hotel of Degas, the Beats and Bertolucci
facts: Built 1870 by the Ben Dahan family, among Tangier's oldest hotels
Guestbook holds Edgar Degas and Winston Churchill
Bertolucci shot scenes of The Sheltering Sky inside
Overlooks the old port from the medina's edge
w: Built 1870 above the harbour, all faded plaster and balconies that have watched the port for a century and a half. Degas and Churchill signed its yellowing book; Bertolucci filmed The Sheltering Sky in its rooms, and Beat poets drifted through. Grand, peeling, gloriously unbothered. Tangier's longest-running stage set.

hook: Art Deco 1930s picture house reborn as North Africa's first cinematheque
facts: Cinema Rif opened on the Grand Socco in 1938
Artist Yto Barrada rescued it in 2006 as the Cinematheque de Tanger
North Africa's first arthouse cinema and film archive
Restored Art Deco facade still anchors the square
w: By the 2000s the 1938 Rif was 600 empty seats showing scratched Bollywood prints to a handful of smokers. Artist Yto Barrada and friends seized it, turning the Art Deco shell into North Africa's first cinematheque and film archive. Now it screens the world from the Grand Socco. A picture palace, dragged back to life.

hook: Cannon-strewn garden where Mohammed V demanded independence
facts: Old cannons dot the lawns, one said to be from Trafalgar
Centuries-old dragon tree and a vast banyan shade the paths
Mohammed V gave his 1947 Tangier Speech on sovereignty here
Once the residence grounds of the sultan's representative
legend: They say the great banyan, the Diplomats' Tree, is some 800 years old
w: Old bronze cannons rust on the lawns, one supposedly hauled from Trafalgar, beneath a dragon tree and a banyan they say has stood 800 years. Here in 1947 Mohammed V stood and called for independence, and the square outside took his date for a name. Empire's leftovers under the Diplomats' Tree. A garden that started a nation.

hook: Graves hewn into the cliff edge, gazing across the strait for 3,000 years
facts: Around a hundred rectangular tombs cut straight into the rock
Dated to roughly 1000 BC, among Tangier's oldest traces
Punic and Roman finds from here sit in the Kasbah Museum
Perched on the Marshan plateau beside Cafe Hafa
w: A hundred-odd graves chiselled body-length into the bare cliff, open to the sky and the strait, cut by Phoenicians some 3,000 years ago. Locals now picnic among them at sunset, feet dangling over the dead. The oldest thing in Tangier, and free to walk. Stone beds carved for the long sleep, facing Spain.

hook: Cliff villa that held 115,000 toy soldiers, now sealed shut
facts: Bought 1970 by publisher Malcolm Forbes as a toy-soldier museum
Held some 115,000 figures staging battles from Waterloo to Dien Bien Phu
Closed after Forbes died in 1990; collection auctioned by Christie's in 1997
Now a Moroccan state guesthouse, shut to the public
w: Magazine tycoon Malcolm Forbes filled this Marshan villa with 115,000 toy soldiers refighting Waterloo and Dien Bien Phu under tiny lights. He threw a legendary birthday bash here in 1989, then died, and Christie's auctioned the armies off. The state took the house and locked the gates. A war museum nobody can enter now.

hook: Tangier's last grand synagogue, hidden behind a plain medina door
facts: Built 1878 by community figure Moshe Nahon
Andalusian carved stucco, embedded columns and trilobe arches inside
Fell derelict, then restored in 1994
Stands on the old Rue des Synagogues near a wall-but-vanished Jewish quarter
w: Behind an anonymous door on the Rue des Synagogues hides 1878 splendour: Andalusian stucco, embedded columns, trilobe arches, the last great prayer hall of a community that has all but left. Built by Moshe Nahon, left to rot, saved in 1994. One lit lamp where a whole quarter once worshipped. Hidden behind a nothing door.
